title: Sideroxylon cartilagineum
taxonomy: Sideroxylon cartilagineum is a species of flowering plant in the family Sapotaceae. This plant is native to the Caribbean and is also known as the Caribbean gumbo-limbo. It is a member of the genus Sideroxylon, which is a group of flowering plants that are found in tropical regions around the world. The genus Sideroxylon is part of the order Ericales, which is a large and diverse group of flowering plants. Sideroxylon cartilagineum is closely related to other species in the Sapotaceae family, such as the sapodilla and the mamey sapote.
uses: Sideroxylon cartilagineum, also known as the West Indian ironwood, has been used in traditional medicine for various purposes. The plant's bark and roots are reported to have antimicrobial properties, which could aid in treating infections and wounds. In some cultures, Sideroxylon cartilagineum is used to treat digestive issues and reduce inflammation. Its medicinal uses may also extend to treating fever and other health conditions, although more research is needed to confirm its effectiveness. Further study is required to fully understand the plant's potential medicinal benefits and applications.

parts: Sideroxylon cartilagineum, also known as the Jamaican dogwood, is a plant used in traditional medicine. The medicinal parts of the Sideroxylon cartilagineum are primarily its bark, leaves, and roots, which have been used to treat various ailments.
